sections.header: Verses 1–15 — “Though Your Sins Are Like Scarlet”

heb.paragraph: "Hear me, you heavens! Listen, earth! The LORD has spoken: I reared children and brought them up, but they have rebelled against me" (v.2). Heaven and earth summoned as witnesses to a covenant lawsuit (rîb). The ox knows its owner; the donkey its master’s manger — but Israel does not know (v.3). Wounds, welts, open sores — not bandaged or soothed (v.6). Why more beatings? (v.5). Sacrifices are meaningless when hands are full of blood (vv.11–15). "Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow" (v.18).

refs.note: Listen, you heavens; hear, you earth — Moses summoned the same witnesses at the covenant’s beginning.
The LORD has a case against his people — the same rîb genre.
The blood of Jesus purifies us from all sin — the NT fulfilment of the scarlet-to-snow promise.
I hate your religious festivals; let justice roll — the same worship-without-justice critique.

notes.note: Heaven and earth as witnesses. The ox knows; the donkey knows; Israel does not. Animals outperform God’s people in basic recognition.
The nation described as a beaten body: wounds, welts, open sores. The metaphor is medical: Judah is critically ill and refusing treatment.
"The multitude of your sacrifices — what are they to me?" God rejects the QUANTITY of worship when the QUALITY of life is corrupt. Hands full of blood cannot offer acceptable sacrifice.
"Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow." The most famous verse in ch.1. The offer of total cleansing stands amid the total indictment.
Calvin reads the ox-and-donkey comparison as devastating: dumb animals recognise their provider; Israel does not recognise its God. The indictment begins with ingratitude, the root of all other sins.

Calvin insists this passage does not reject sacrifice (which God commanded) but sacrifice offered by bloodstained hands. The problem is not liturgy but hypocrisy. Calvin applies this to any church that substitutes ceremony for righteousness.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the verb "reared" (giddal) implies parental investment: God raised Israel from infancy to adulthood. The rebellion is not stranger-against-stranger but child-against-parent.
The NET observes the conditional grammar: "IF you are willing and obedient" (v.19). The scarlet-to-snow transformation requires response; it is offered, not imposed.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt emphasises the covenant-lawsuit framework: the heavens and earth were witnesses at Sinai (Deut 4:26); now they are summoned to testify. The entire cosmos is God’s courtroom.
Oswalt reads the sacrifice-rejection as the prophetic insistence that cult without character is abomination. The sacrificial system was designed to EXPRESS a relationship, not REPLACE one.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads ch.1 as the canonical introduction to the entire 66-chapter book: it establishes the themes (judgment, sin, offer of grace) that will be developed across First, Second, and Third Isaiah. The chapter is a miniature of the whole.
Childs notes the scarlet-to-snow promise functions proleptically: it anticipates the servant’s atoning work (ch.53) and the new creation (ch.65). The offer made here is fulfilled THERE.

hist.context: Isaiah opens not with his call (that comes in ch.6) but with God’s legal case against Judah. The covenant-lawsuit (rîb) genre is well attested in the prophets (Mic 6:1–8; Hos 4:1–3): heaven and earth are summoned as witnesses because they were present at the original covenant (Deut 30:19; 32:1). The date is the reigns of Uzziah through Hezekiah (c.740–700 BC). Judah is prosperous but morally bankrupt. The sacrificial critique (vv.11–15) does not reject ritual per se but ritual divorced from justice.

sections.header: Verses 16–31 — “The Faithful City Has Become a Prostitute”

heb.paragraph: "See how the faithful city has become a prostitute! She once was full of justice; righteousness (ṣədāqâ) used to dwell in her — but now murderers!" (v.21). The prescription: wash yourselves, make yourselves clean; stop doing wrong, learn to do right; seek justice, defend the oppressed (vv.16–17). Zion will be delivered with justice, her penitent ones with righteousness (v.27). But rebels and sinners will be destroyed (v.28). They will be ashamed of the sacred oaks and gardens they chose (v.29).

refs.note: I planted you as a choice vine — but you have become a corrupt, wild vine — the same faithful-turned-unfaithful imagery.
You used your beauty and became a prostitute — Ezekiel’s extended version of Isa 1:21.
Pure religion is to look after orphans and widows — the NT version of Isa 1:17’s prescription.
Nothing impure will enter the new Jerusalem — the ultimate fulfilment of the purified-city vision.

notes.note: "Wash yourselves. Stop doing wrong. Learn to do right. Seek justice. Defend the oppressed." Five imperatives: the prescription after the diagnosis.
"The faithful city has become a prostitute!" The sharpest single-verse summary of Judah’s apostasy.
"I will turn my hand against you; I will thoroughly purge away your dross." Judgment as purification, not destruction. God refines; he does not discard.
"Zion will be delivered with justice." The promise embedded in the judgment: deliverance comes, but it comes THROUGH justice, not apart from it.
Calvin calls these the clearest ethical imperatives in Isaiah: not mystical directives but plain moral commands. The religion God wants is justice enacted, not ritual performed.

Calvin reads the refining-fire metaphor as the theology of redemptive suffering: God’s judgment burns away sin, not the sinner. The dross is removed; the silver remains. This is hope disguised as threat.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "prostitute" (zonah) is legal-covenant language: Israel’s exclusive relationship with YHWH (the "marriage") has been violated by other loyalties.

The NET observes the wordplay: Zion redeemed with mishpat (justice) and ṣədāqâ (righteousness) — the same pair that was supposed to characterise her in the first place (v.21). God restores what was lost.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt notes the imperatives move from negative ("stop") to positive ("learn," "seek," "defend"). Repentance is not merely ceasing evil but actively pursuing good. The ethical life is not passive.

Oswalt reads the refining as the programme for the entire book: Isaiah traces how God purifies Judah through judgment (chs.1–39), exile (ch.40ff), and ultimately through the Servant’s suffering (ch.53).

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs sees the prostitute-city / purified-city contrast as the book’s narrative arc in miniature: unfaithful Jerusalem (ch.1) → judged Jerusalem (chs.2–39) → comforted Jerusalem (chs.40–55) → glorified Jerusalem (chs.60–66). The chapter is a map of the entire book.

Childs notes that "redeemed with justice" holds together what later theology sometimes separates: redemption IS justice, not an alternative to it. God’s saving act is simultaneously his righteous act.

hist.context: The "faithful city become a prostitute" metaphor (v.21) launches the infidelity-imagery that Hosea, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel develop extensively. The term ṣədāqâ (righteousness/justice) appears five times in this chapter — it is Isaiah’s master-concept, meaning both ethical conduct and the right-ordering of society. The "sacred oaks and gardens" (v.29) refer to Canaanite fertility worship practised alongside YHWH worship. The chapter ends with fire imagery: the strong man becomes tinder, his work a spark (v.31).

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: Israel is a key figure in The Great Arraignment.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVUnless the Lord Almighty had left us some survivors, we would have become like Sodom, we would have been like Gomorrah.ESVIf the LORD of hosts had not left us a few survivors, we should have been like Sodom, and become like Gomorrah.NIVDaughter Zion is left like a shelter in a vineyard, like a hut in a cucumber field, like a city under siege.ESVAnd the daughter of Zion is left like a booth in a vineyard, like a lodge in a cucumber field, like a besieged city.

src.title: The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BC)
src.quote: The Cyrus Cylinder relates to Deutero-Isaiah (chapters 40-55), not Isaiah chapter 1 being discussed here.
src.note: The cylinder confirms Isaiah’s prophecy of a Persian king who would liberate captives and authorize temple rebuilding, whether the prophecy is dated early (8th c.) or late (6th c.).

src.title: The Sennacherib Prism (c.700 BC)
src.quote: The Sennacherib Prism relates to events described in Isaiah 36-39, not Isaiah chapter 1.
src.note: The silence confirms Isaiah 36–37: Sennacherib besieged but never conquered Jerusalem. His own records inadvertently verify Isaiah’s narrative of divine deliverance.

src.title: The Great Isaiah Scroll (1QIsaᵃ, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A complete copy of Isaiah found at Qumran, dated c.125 BC. The oldest complete biblical manuscript, it is virtually identical to the Masoretic text 1,000 years later.
src.note: The scroll demonstrates the remarkable textual stability of Isaiah across a millennium. The text the church reads today is essentially the text the Qumran community read.

rec.text: "Heaven and earth as witnesses. The ox knows; the donkey knows; Israel does not. Animals outperform God’s people in basic recognition."

rows.label: The Prostitute City — 1:16–31
rows.text: "The faithful city has become a PROSTITUTE." Five imperatives. "Zion will be delivered with JUSTICE."

hebtext.note: "Hear me, you heavens! Listen, earth! The LORD has spoken: I reared children and brought them up, but they have rebelled against me" (v.2). Heaven and earth summoned as witnesses to a covenant lawsuit (rîb). The ox knows its owner; the donkey its master’s manger — but Israel does not know (v.3). Wounds, welts, open sores — not bandaged or soothed (v.6). Why more beatings? (v.5). Sacrifices are meaningless when hands are full of blood (vv.11–15). "Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow" (v.18).

"See how the faithful city has become a prostitute! She once was full of justice; righteousness (ṣədāqâ) used to dwell in her — but now murderers!" (v.21). The prescription: wash yourselves, make yourselves clean; stop doing wrong, learn to do right; seek justice, defend the oppressed (vv.16–17). Zion will be delivered with justice, her penitent ones with righteousness (v.27). But rebels and sinners will be destroyed (v.28). They will be ashamed of the sacred oaks and gardens they chose (v.29).

thread.text: Listen, you heavens; hear, you earth — Moses summoned the same witnesses at the covenant’s beginning.
The LORD has a case against his people — the same rîb genre.
The blood of Jesus purifies us from all sin — the NT fulfilment of the scarlet-to-snow promise.
I hate your religious festivals; let justice roll — the same worship-without-justice critique.
I planted you as a choice vine — but you have become a corrupt, wild vine — the same faithful-turned-unfaithful imagery.
You used your beauty and became a prostitute — Ezekiel’s extended version of Isa 1:21.

themes.note: Covenant and Judgment and Justice all at 9: the great arraignment. God’s legal case against his own people. Scarlet to snow is the mercy inside the judgment.

coaching.tip: Isaiah's opening is a covenant lawsuit: heaven and earth are summoned as witnesses (v. 2), echoing Deuteronomy 32. God charges Israel with rebellion worse than animals — 'the ox knows its owner, but Israel does not know.' This legal framework structures the first part of the book. God isn't merely disappointed; he's prosecuting a case.

The remedy offered (vv. 16-20) is surprising: not more sacrifices but justice — 'learn to do good; seek justice, correct oppression.' Religion without ethics is worse than useless. Then comes the stunning promise: 'though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow.' Isaiah holds together God's demand for righteousness and his offer of cleansing.
